cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

burial.plot_notes: South Burying Ground, Kensington, Hartford County, Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth died young; the year was not recorded.

cross_connections.display_label: His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.
His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.

military_service.notes: Served under Capts. Selah Hart and James Stoddard, Col. Wolcott's regiment.

narrative_blocks.header: Born three months after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: His father Matthew Hart died on October 30, 1736; he was born January 23, 1737 — a posthumous child. He grew up in Kensington and joined the church there in October 1760 at age twenty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage created double Hooker descent
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Hart III married Urania Hooker — both Hooker descendants. Their grandchildren carried double descent from Thomas Hooker through different family lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed his household to New York around 1800
narrative_blocks.body: Around the turn of the nineteenth century he sold the Sheldon Moore homestead in Kensington and moved to Madison County, New York — to either Whitestown or De Ruyter. He died there around 1811, more than a decade after the move.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became America's foremost 1820s poet
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson James Gates Percival (1795-1856) — son of daughter Elizabeth and Dr. James Percival Jr. — became the most recognized American poet of the 1820s and Connecticut's first state geologist. James was born in Kensington before Matthew Hart Jr. departed for New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Born after his father had died
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Hart was born in January 1737, weeks after his father died the previous October. He grew up to serve as a Revolutionary corporal and later removed to New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1784; ordained by Bishop Seabury; Hempstead rector
narrative_blocks.detail: He served as agent for the Connecticut Land Company (1796-1797) surveying lands in Cleveland, Ohio. Among his boarding school students around 1809 was his nephew James Gates Percival (X01936) — son of his sister Elizabeth Hart (H00610) — who later became a noted poet, Connecticut state geologist, and Wisconsin state geologist, writing his final report from memory without notes. The Hofstra University library holds the Hart-Nichols family papers, including correspondence compiled by Seth's granddaughter Mary Amelia Hart (1838-1932). His portrait was painted by Edwin Percival (nephew James Gates's brother) around 1829. (Hart Genealogy 1948; Hofstra Hart-Nichols Family Papers)
narrative_blocks.body: Seth Hart graduated Yale in 1784 and was ordained by Bishop Samuel Seabury — deacon in 1791, priest in 1792. He served as rector of St. George's Church in Hempstead, Long Island for 28 years (1800-1829), until paralysis forced his resignation. He died in 1832, 'much beloved and lamented by his former parishioners.'

narrative_blocks.header: His boarding school launched nephew Percival's poetry
narrative_blocks.body: He ran a boarding school at Hempstead where his nephew James Gates Percival was sent in 1807 after their father's death. Despite Percival's resentment of his uncle's 'amiable, cheerful, almost jovial' efforts to draw him out, the Hempstead years were where Percival first wrote poetry.

narrative_blocks.header: Officiated Cleveland's first baptism, marriage, and burial
narrative_blocks.body: In spring 1797 he went to the Western Reserve of Ohio as General Agent and Chaplain of the surveying party sent by the Connecticut Land Company to develop their settlement at Cleveland. He spent six months in service and officiated at the first baptism, the first marriage, and the first burial occurring in Cleveland.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1784; rector St. George's Hempstead 28 years

cross_connections.display_label: His boarding school was where nephew Percival first wrote poetry
His portrait painted by his nephew Edwin Percival — a Hooker descendant

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale 1784; became Episcopal rector at Hempstead LI for 28 years.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Episcopal rector at Hempstead, Long Island

career.organization: St. George's Church, Hempstead, Long Island
career.notes: Earlier rector of St. John's, Waterbury and St. Paul's, Wallingford. Yale 1784; ordained by Bishop Seabury.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of poet-geologist James Gates Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart Percival's son James Gates Percival (born September 15, 1795) became a major American poet and the official state geologist of both Connecticut and Wisconsin after his Yale 1815 graduation. Elizabeth herself was described as 'by nature of a susceptible and delicate organization' who 'seems to have imparted to her son these qualities, with a tendency to excessive mental development.' She was understood to have transmitted depression and extreme sensitivity to her sons — James also suffered from depression throughout his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost husband and daughter within five weeks, 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth's first husband Dr. James Percival Jr. died January 22, 1807 in Kensington Connecticut at age forty, and five weeks later her daughter Harriet died February 16, 1807 at age seventeen. The compressed loss of husband and eldest child within five weeks marked the family's catastrophic early-1807. Elizabeth waited five years before remarrying — to Samuel Porter on April 6, 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James worked Porter farm before re-entering Yale
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1812 second marriage to Samuel Porter, Elizabeth's son James Gates Percival worked on Porter's farm before re-entering Yale to complete his 1815 degree. The interim farm labor between the family rebuild and resumption of Yale studies was the kind of practical stop-gap that interrupted many early-Republic professional-track educations after parental deaths reset family finances.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Kensington 1769, four children with Dr. Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart was born in 1769 in Kensington Connecticut (baptized December 24, 1769) and married Dr. James Percival Jr. there in January 1790 at age twenty. Four children survived to be documented: Harriet (died 1807 age 17), Edwin (died Troy NY), James Gates Percival (Yale 1815 poet-geologist), and Oswin Hart Percival (baptized 1809, died never married at a New Haven hospital). Elizabeth herself died around 1846.

open_questions.question: Identity of unnamed Hart daughter (H00612)
open_questions.notes: EH entry 612: 'Daughter, born ___; single. Lived with her sister, Mrs. Percival.' This is a sibling of Elizabeth Hart Percival (H00610) — one of Matthew Hart Jr.'s daughters who never married and lived with Elizabeth. Not to be confused with Harriet Percival (X01940), who was Elizabeth's daughter and died in 1807 age 17. No name found in Hart Genealogy 1948, EH, or FindAGrave. FindAGrave lists a 'Timothy Percival (1776-1808)' as a child of Elizabeth Hart — likely a FindAGrave error (Elizabeth was born 1769; a child born 1776 at age 7 is implausible). Candidate sources: Kensington church records, Berlin probate.
open_questions.action: Check Kensington Congregational Church records and Berlin CT probate for unnamed Hart daughter

parents.notes: Son of John Hart (X01887) who perished in the 1666 fire

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID: 77180356.

narrative_blocks.header: Away tending stock the night his family died
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1666 a fire set to the Hart house in Farmington killed everyone inside; John, then about eleven, survived only because he had been sent that night to Nod to tend the family's stock. He grew up the sole survivor of his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Three grandsons wed three of Hooker’s granddaughters
narrative_blocks.body: John's grandfather, Deacon Stephen Hart, had served Rev. Thomas Hooker's own church in Hartford. A generation later, three of John's grandsons — the brothers Matthew, Samuel, and Nathaniel Hart — married three granddaughters of Thomas Hooker, knotting the two families together within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandfather’s will singled the orphan out
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Stephen Hart wrote his will in 1682, he named the fire's lone survivor directly as his eldest son's son. The boy who had lost everyone kept his place in the family record and a small legacy.

cross_connections.display_label: Their families united — three sons married three Hooker daughters.

burialCemetery.name: Christian Lane Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Kensington is a parish of Berlin, CT — sources may list burial location as either 'Kensington' or 'Berlin'. John Hooker No. 2 (H00098) and Mercy Hart Hooker (I00041) buried here. Burial site of Samuel Hooker (H00086) and family
